HubSpot is a powerful platform, but in day-to-day use it can be difficult to maneuver, especially when working with large, multi-state account lists.

One of the biggest challenges is the lack of simple sorting and filtering by state. Tasks that should be straightforwardâ€”such as quickly viewing accounts by geographic regionâ€”often require workarounds, custom properties, or advanced filters that arenâ€™t intuitive.

Reporting is another pain point. Creating meaningful, customized reports can feel overly complex and time-consuming. The report builder has a learning curve, and even after investing time, it can be difficult to generate reports that clearly reflect real-world sales activity without significant manual effort.

Overall, HubSpot has strong capabilities, but the user experience around navigation, list management, and reporting could be more streamlinedâ€”especially for teams that rely heavily on territory-based selling and data visibility.

Response Text: Cannot really see how hubspot is helping us.

We are a nonprofit rescue mission serving individuals experiencing homelessness by providing essential services such as shelter, food, and clothing. Our internal staff rely heavily on HubSpot forms to enter data and accurately capture the services we provide.

Unfortunately, we have encountered multiple instances where HubSpot updates or configuration issues have made our work more difficult and, at times, compromised the integrity of our data. For example, for several months all of our form submissions were incorrectly marked as spam because they originated from our organizationâ€™s IP address, which had been excluded during the initial onboarding setup.

Additionally, we continue to experience issues where contact records are overwritten when staff submit forms for different individuals. Even when using the correct unique identifiers and properly configured forms, HubSpot appears to associate submissions with the wrong contact, resulting in data being overwritten on existing records. This requires manual investigation and correction by our team, which is both time-consuming and frustrating.

These issues directly impact our ability to reliably track services and serve our clients effectively.

Response Text: Needs a more holistic view and workflow that is seamless, easy to move through

The mission HubSpot is onâ€”to bring all GTM actions into one placeâ€”makes it a no-brainer. Ease of use, combined with true customization, makes it a thrill to use and to drive adoption. Iâ€™m giving 10/10 because HubSpot genuinely feels like the control room for the entire commercial motion, from first touch to renewal and expansion. Few platforms manage to be both simple for the team and deep for the operators; HubSpot does, and it shows up in speed, clarity, and better decisions.

What Iâ€™d love to see next is an even bolder push into post-sales. Thereâ€™s huge untapped value in the workflows that happen after the contract is signed, and HubSpot is perfectly positioned to own that space. Start with account hierarchy done right: parentâ€“child structures where health, NPS, revenue (NRR/GRR), product adoption, and even pipeline can roll up cleanly to the parent, with permissions and views that follow the tree. Parent-level reporting should be plug-and-playâ€”one snapshot that surfaces top risks, contract landscape, renewal timeline, and open actions without requiring a dozen custom reports.

Playbooks are already useful, but theyâ€™re ready for a leap. Imagine Playbooks that branch dynamically based on context like plan, MRR, persona, lifecycle stage, or recent sentiment, and that pre-fill from CRM, log key decisions, create follow-ups, and update the success plan with one click. Add versioning, A/B testing, and clear outcome analytics so we can see which playbooks correlate with better health and higher NRR, and make it easy to share best-practice templates across teams and regions with granular access.

AI should be built into post-sales, not bolted on. Meeting and conversation context ought to flow directly into health scoringâ€”if a callâ€™s tone is off, if â€œblocked,â€ â€œdelayed,â€ or â€œconfusedâ€ keeps popping up, or if a customer goes unusually quiet, that should move the needle. Onboarding intelligence should track milestones and time-to-value automatically and flag slippage early. Relationship maps should emerge from mail and calendar signals to help us maintain stakeholder coverage. And EBRs should be one click away: a deck assembled from real usage, outcomes, risks, and next steps.

Give us a health-score builder that normal humans can own. Let us weight signals like product usage, support load, sentiment, value milestones, and financials with clear, editable thresholds. Include industry templates to get started fast and keep the scores explainable so a CSM can say exactly why a customer moved from yellow to green.

Renewals and expansion need to live where CS actually works. A single renewals board for CSMs with contracts, risk signals, stakeholder maps, suggested next actions, and a clean handoff into Deals when it makes sense. And let CSQLs flow natively into Sales Hub with transparent criteria and measurable impactâ€”we run CSQL as a discipline and it pays off when itâ€™s seamless inside the CRM.

One more thing that matters at scale: partners. Many of us operate through partner channels. Treat the partner as the parent with multiple client accounts beneath and offer partner scorecards, co-sell loops, and adoption dashboards by partner. That unlocks serious growth.

Round it out with governance and performance. Playbook lifecycle management with draftâ€“reviewâ€“publish, gentle nudges for field hygiene and consolidation, faster large tables, inline edits in reports, and smoother commenting on activities. I know some of this can be stitched together today with code and third-party tools, but having it native and opinionated in the UI would be a game-changer.

In short: HubSpot is already the best place to run the full GTM engine. Push harder into post-sales, nail the parentâ€“child model, and let AI truly understand context, and youâ€™ll turn a great platform into the obvious operating system for durable growth. Keep goingâ€”Iâ€™m all in.

There are plenty of other issues that my team and I experience frequently that make our roles more difficult, but these are the ones that are most top-of-mind. 

If given the choice, I would never voluntarily utilize Hubspot as a CRM tool, and have had much better experiences with tools like Zendesk and Salesforce.

My main concern is with the data visualization tool used to create dashboards in HubSpot. The tool is quite limited in terms of functionality, and even within those limitations, it seems to have issues with advanced filters. Some of these filters only work at the report level but not at the dashboard level. For example, filters like "date range" (e.g., "today") sometimes show only today's data in some reports, while in others, they display data from the past 24 hours in advanced reports or CJRs.

If HubSpot has these limitations in data analytics, it would be very helpful if they provided a native connector to Power BI or Qlik Sense to facilitate data export and analysis. Currently, most connectors are offered by third-party companies at a high cost.

Response Text:
HS has excellent functionality. However, I do not like how you are focusing companies on an Enterprise level for SMBs, as they also need functionality. You have pricing that is out of the scope for SMBs or a more significant gap that needs to be addressed to help develop the SMB business. You have the functionality all companies need at any level to grow, but you are placing it all in an unaffordable enterprise group. Help companies adopt the features as they grow with achievable pricing to match the company's size. Salesforce once did this, and now they are going in the opposite direction to SMBs and. HS seems to be focused only on Enterprise clients and offers impressive functionality that can truly benefit businesses of any size. However, it seems that the current focus is primarily on Enterprise-level solutions, which can alienate small and medium-sized businesses (SMBs) that also require robust features to succeed. 

The pricing structure appears to be out of reach for many SMBs, creating a significant barrier to accessing the tools they need for growth. I believe there is an opportunity to better serve this segment by offering more flexible pricing that scales with the company's size and needs. 

Take Salesforce as an example; they initially catered to a broad range of businesses but have shifted their focus more recently. It would be great to see HS devise a strategy that embraces SMBs, providing them with the same level of functionality that larger enterprises enjoy but with pricing that reflects their unique circumstances. This would not only help these companies adopt essential features as they grow but also foster a loyal customer base that sees HS as a valuable partner in their journey.

I'm in tech, and I'm great at Salesforce, Tableau, Monday, Workday etc. so I'm not a rookie when it comes to things like this. A YEAR AGO I learned that I could probably get a HubSpot certification in about 4 hours. The purpose is for my rÃ©sumÃ©, not to grow my business. So, I thought WTH...let's.

After about 2 hours and learning that, instead of HubSpot providing me with a fake sales team, I could only create a team by sending email invitations to fake email addresses that I created, then log in to those, accept the invite, an create a profile for each...I was disheartened. In trying to proceed without a team, I hit another roadblock. The dashboard (more specifically the options) that the instructor what demonstrating was nothing like my dashboard, so I could not click on the same options. So, I cut my losses and left.

Here I am almost a year later. I bit the bullet, created fake email addresses. Created fake profiles. Created my team. Watched half the videos again, and...SAME ROADBLOCK. The instructors recommend opening a separate browser and following along on our dashboard. However, on "Learning Your HubSpot Starter Tools" >> "Increase Your Efficiency..." the very first action "click on Conversations..." is not even a possibility for me, because my dashboard looks completely different from what they're showing.

After learning that your Chatbot is nothing more than an animation that says, "navigate our website and try to find an answer" after wasting my time and giving me false hope...I eventually manage to find a way to talk to your team. Juan...Juan (Milano??) did not care that I sent screen shots of my issue. He did not let me finish explaining the issue (which in my experience is greatly appreciated by troubleshooters). He said, "please click on the left. go to Reports". I did that, awaiting further instruction on how to solve my issue. Then he says, "can I help you with anything else?"

WTF!?

He proceeded to spend more time arguing with me, that he had solved my issue, rather than trying to understand that my issue was not that I did not know how to navigate a side menu and find "Reports" but thatâ€”now that I'm there, I do not have a menu option that says, "Conversations" (or anything). So, I cannot follow along with the lesson. He then proceeds to tell me, "we changed the menu! So there!" as if he'd solved my problems. 

So, here I am, 4 more hours later, worse for wear, and back to where I left off a year ago. 

I don't understand why you offer an Academy that doesn't have all the learning components available (e.g. mock team members). I understand that you want to reserve some live help for paying customers, but man...this????! This is horrible. In order to learn your product, I have to want it bad enough to create a bunch of dummy email addresses, go to forums and post questions that might be answered within 24 hours, then come back to respond...I mean, it's just not worth it!
